title: Introduction to ARM Assembly
summary: Understand what assembly language is, why it matters, and how ARM fits in the processor landscape.
content_html:
What Is Assembly Programming?

Assembly language is the lowest level of human-readable programming. Every processor family has its own assembly language - Intel Core, ARM, MIPS, etc. The assembler translates assembly code into binary machine instructions (bit strings that the processor directly executes).

Programming Language Hierarchy

High Level:   Python, Java, C# (interpreted/compiled)
Mid Level:    C, C++, Pascal (compiled to native)
Low Level:    Assembly language (1-to-1 with machine code)
Machine Code: Binary instructions (0s and 1s)

Assembly has a near 1-to-1 mapping with machine instructions. One assembly instruction typically produces one machine instruction.

Why Learn Assembly?

  • Write tiny/fast programs - bare metal with no OS overhead
  • No operating system required - direct hardware access
  • Write compilers and drivers - understand what C compiles to
  • Find vulnerabilities - security research and reverse engineering
  • Reverse engineering - all binaries can be disassembled back to assembly
  • Hardware-specific programming - embedded systems, microcontrollers

RISC vs CISC

Processor instruction sets fall into two categories:

CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)  | RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)
Example: Intel x86                        | Example: ARM
Emphasis on hardware                      | Emphasis on software
Many complex specialised instructions     | Small set of simple instructions
Variable cycle time per instruction       | Typically one cycle per instruction
More transistors for complex instructions | More transistors for registers
Higher power usage                        | Lower power usage
Suited to multimedia/desktop              | Suited to mobile/embedded/IoT

ARM Architecture

ARM = Advanced RISC Machine. It is the most widely used processor architecture in the world:

  • Over 130 billion ARM processors produced
  • Found in: mobile devices, routers, IoT devices, Raspberry Pi, Arduino
  • About 100 instructions total (we will study a subset)
  • 32-bit registers (ARMv7) or 64-bit (ARMv8)

Bare Metal Programming

In this unit we write bare metal assembly - code that runs directly on the hardware without an operating system. This means:

  • No file system, no scheduler, no OS calls
  • You control every instruction the CPU executes
  • Programs are loaded directly onto the microSD card and run at boot
  • We use the FASMARM assembler to compile .asm files to binary

FASMARM Assembler

FASMARM (Flat Assembler for ARM) is a simple one-step assembler we use in this unit:

  • No .section, _start, .global declarations needed
  • Native support for named constants and macros
  • No Makefiles - simple GUI compile button
  • Supports ARM6 (RPi 1), ARM7 (RPi 2), ARMv8 (RPi 3/4)
